title: Te Whakairo o Te Awa: Carving the River's Story / Understanding Cultural Context Through Whakairo
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (Māori carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu that flows near our college. The awa holds stories of navigation, sustenance, and spiritual significance that are traditionally expressed through carving
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret the cultural narratives embedded in traditional whakairo patterns specific to Ngāti Kahungunu
Design and create a contemporary whakairo artwork that tells a personal or community story connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Apply understanding of symmetry, negative space, and traditional Māori design principles in a practical carving project
Present and explain the cultural significance and personal meaning of their created whakairo to peers

activities.title: He Kōrero mō Te Awa: Story of the Stream
activities.description: Visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua (elder) to hear traditional stories and learn about the significance of patterns found in nature (ripples, fish scales, water flow) that influence Ngāti Kahungunu whakairo designs

activities.title: Te Whakapapa o te Whakairo: Lineage of Carving
activities.description: Hands-on workshop with a local carver to learn basic whakairo techniques using soap or soft wood, focusing on patterns inspired by the awa (kōruru/taniwha, unaunahi/fish scales, pūhoro/water currents)

activities.title: He Whakaaro Hou: Contemporary Interpretations
activities.description: Students research contemporary Māori artists (e.g., Sandy Adsett, Kohai Grace) who blend traditional patterns with modern themes, then sketch designs for their own whakairo piece that tells a personal story connected to the local environment

activities.title: Te Mahi Whakairo: The Carving Practice
activities.description: Practical session where students transfer their designs to medium-density fiberboard and use lino cutting tools to create their contemporary whakairo prints, focusing on technical skill and cultural authenticity

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga: Gallery Presentation
activities.description: Students curate a classroom gallery exhibition of their whakairo prints, presenting their work in both English and te reo Māori, explaining the cultural narratives and personal connections to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu to share oral histories of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Traditional Whakairo by Cliff Whiting (book) from college library
Te Papa Collections Online - Māori carving database
Local carver Hemi Sundgren for practical workshops

tasks: Completed contemporary whakairo print on MDF board
Written artist statement explaining cultural significance and design choices
Oral presentation in te reo Māori and English describing their artwork

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Investigate the characteristics, uses, and purposes of objects and images from past and present cultures
Developing Practical Knowledge: Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles for specific purposes
Communicating and Interpreting: Describe and respond to the ways in which objects and images communicate meaning
Developing Ideas: Develop and refine ideas in response to a variety of motivations and cultural contexts
